title: Dog Hyperactive After Eating Coffee Grounds: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your dog is hyperactive after eating coffee grounds, they may have caffeine poisoning. This is a high-urgency emergency requiring immediate care.
content_html:
TL;DR: Ingesting coffee grounds is a high-urgency medical emergency for dogs that requires immediate veterinary attention to prevent life-threatening cardiovascular and neurological complications.

What is caffeine toxicity in dogs?

Caffeine belongs to a group of chemicals called methylxanthines. While humans can process caffeine easily, dogs are much more sensitive to its effects. When a dog consumes coffee grounds, the caffeine is rapidly absorbed into their bloodstream, causing severe overstimulation of the central nervous system, the cardiovascular system, and the kidneys.

Is caffeine ingestion a medical emergency for my dog?

  • Yes, this is a High Urgency situation that requires immediate veterinary intervention.
  • Hyperactivity is one of the first clinical signs that your dog has ingested a toxic dose.
  • Because coffee grounds are concentrated, even small amounts can cause life-threatening symptoms like an elevated heart rate, tremors, seizures, and cardiac arrest.
  • Contact an emergency veterinarian or a pet poison control center immediately.

How can taking a photo help the vet triage my dog?

  • If safe to do so, take a photo of the coffee packaging to help the vet identify the specific caffeine concentration.
  • Photograph the remaining grounds or the spill to help the medical team estimate the total volume ingested.
  • This information allows the veterinary team to calculate the exact level of risk and move straight to the most effective treatment.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Following ingestion of coffee grounds, which contain methylxanthines, emergency triage should focus on decontamination and stabilization. If the animal is stable or presenting within one hour of ingestion, emesis should be induced using apomorphine or hydrogen peroxide; gastric lavage may be considered in sedated animals. Administer activated charcoal (1-4 g/kg, PO), and repeat every 12 hours in symptomatic animals due to enterohepatic recirculation, controlling vomiting with metoclopramide (0.2-0.4 mg/kg, SC or IM, qid, as needed). Symptomatic treatment includes thermoregulation, correction of acid-base and electrolyte abnormalities, electrocardiographic monitoring of cardiac status, and urinary catheter placement to prevent reabsorption of methylxanthines across the bladder wall. For tremors or mild seizures, administer diazepam (0.5-2 mg/kg, slow IV); barbiturates may be needed for severe seizures. Treat arrhythmias as needed with propranolol (0.02-0.06 mg/kg, slow IV) or metoprolol (0.2-0.4 mg/kg, slow IV) for tachyarrhythmias, atropine (0.01-0.02 mg/kg) for bradyarrhythmias, and lidocaine (1-2 mg/kg, IV, followed by 25-80 mcg/kg/min infusion) for refractory ventricular tachyarrhythmias. Fluid diuresis may assist cardiovascular function and urinary excretion. Clinical signs may persist for up to 72 hours in severe cases.

Chapter: Toxicology, Emergency, Cardiology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 2967)

faqs.question: How quickly do symptoms of caffeine poisoning appear?
faqs.answer: Symptoms typically begin to manifest within 30 to 60 minutes of ingestion.

faqs.question: What are the common signs of caffeine toxicity in dogs?
faqs.answer: Common signs include extreme restlessness, rapid breathing, heart palpitations, muscle tremors, and vomiting.

faqs.question: Should I try to make my dog vomit at home?
faqs.answer: Do not induce vomiting unless specifically instructed to do so by a licensed veterinarian, as this can be dangerous if the dog is already showing neurological symptoms.

faqs.question: Is decaf coffee safe for dogs?
faqs.answer: While decaf contains significantly less caffeine, it still contains trace amounts and should be avoided entirely to prevent any risk of upset stomach or toxicity.
